summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the primary energy source that fuels hurricanes?

Hurricanes derive their energy from warm ocean water (at least 26.5 degrees C); evaporation from the warm surface transfers moisture and latent heat into the atmosphere, which is released when the water vapor condenses, fueling the storm's circulation.

What is a weather front?

A weather front is the boundary zone between two air masses of different temperature, humidity, and density; fronts are often associated with changes in weather, including cloud formation, precipitation, and wind shifts.

What is the 'dew point'?

The dew point is the temperature to which air must be cooled (at constant pressure) for it to become saturated and for water vapor to begin condensing into liquid droplets; it is an indicator of atmospheric moisture content.

What is a 'low-pressure system'?

A low-pressure system (cyclone) is an area where atmospheric pressure is lower than surrounding regions, causing air to converge at the surface and rise; rising air cools and condenses, typically producing clouds, precipitation, and unsettled weather.

What is the meaning of 'air mass'?

An air mass is a large volume of air that has acquired relatively uniform temperature and moisture characteristics from the surface over which it formed (its source region), and it retains these properties as it moves.

A Category 4 hurricane is approaching a low-lying coastal city. The National Weather Service predicts a storm surge of 4-5 meters. City officials debate whether to order an evacuation of neighborhoods below 3 meters elevation. What is the greatest immediate threat to residents who remain in these low-lying areas?

Storm surge is historically the greatest cause of hurricane-related deaths; a 4-5 meter surge would completely inundate areas below 3 meters elevation, creating life-threatening flood conditions that make escape extremely dangerous or impossible.

A local news reporter claims that a blizzard in January proves that global warming is not real. An atmospheric scientist responds by explaining why this claim is incorrect. What is the most scientifically accurate explanation?

Climate refers to long-term patterns, not individual weather events; global warming increases ocean evaporation, which can provide more moisture for winter storms, potentially producing heavier snowfalls even as average global temperatures rise over decades.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A small island nation in the Pacific reports that extreme rainfall events have increased by 30% over the past 50 years, while total average annual rainfall has remained nearly the same. What climate-related phenomenon best explains why storms are more intense even though total precipitation is unchanged?

The Clausius-Clapeyron relationship shows that a warmer atmosphere can hold approximately 7% more water vapor per degree Celsius of warming; this means that when precipitation does occur, it can be more intense, even if the overall annual total remains similar.
